policy_statement: Study technical, economic and emissions impacts of hybrid heating systems (heat pump + district heating), verify existing systems, and produce recommendations for efficient connections and implementation (budget €150,000).
mechanism_description: The policy will study hybrid heat pump–district heating systems and create implementation recommendations, helping enable efficient low-carbon heating connections that can reduce building heating emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Heating system planning/technical guidance → accelerated low-carbon heat deployment (heat pumps/district heat integration) → ↓ fossil heat use → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: city/utility/building owners receive technical-economic-emissions assessment and connection/implementation recommendations for hybrid heat pump–district heating configurations, reducing uncertainty and barriers to investment/connection decisions.  
(2) Mediating physical process: adoption of heat pumps and optimized district heating connections increases use of electricity/low-carbon network heat and improves system efficiency, displacing on-site fossil boilers or higher-carbon heat production.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: lower direct (and potentially indirect) CO₂e emissions from building heating due to reduced fossil fuel combustion and improved heat supply efficiency/carbon intensity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling a switch in building heat supply toward lower-carbon sources, leading to reduced operational GHG emissions from heating.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: knowledge/planning support enables low-carbon heating deployment, which primarily reduces operational GHG emissions from buildings.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while efficiency improvements may occur, the central climate benefit is emissions mitigation via fuel/heat-source switching and decarbonized heat supply; it is not chiefly about reducing energy input per se (Resource Efficiency) nor reducing hazard exposure (Adaptation) nor enhancing ecosystems (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: if the district heating is supplied by fossil CHP/boilers and the study only improves connections without reducing carbon intensity, the effect could lean toward efficiency; however the stated intent is “low-carbon heating connections” and hybrid heat pumps, making mitigation the dominant expected outcome for this mechanism.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: any policy framed as feasibility study/recommendations explicitly intended to enable deployment of low-carbon heating (hybrid heat pump + district heating integration) has its main climate-relevant effect through emissions reductions from decarbonizing heat supply.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether the realized impact is mostly fuel-switching vs efficiency gains vs grid interaction depends on local heat-source emissions factors and resulting deployment, which must be assessed at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If electricity is high-carbon or district heat is fossil-heavy and hybrids increase electricity use without decarbonization, near-term emissions benefits could be smaller or ambiguous; nevertheless, the mechanism’s dominant intended pathway is enabling low-carbon heat deployment (mitigation). Also note this is an enabling/planning action—emissions reductions are indirect and contingent on subsequent implementation.
key_indicators: “will be studied”; “technical, economical and emission-related impacts”; “recommendations will be created”; “hybrid heating system (heat pump + district heating)”; “Cost estimate: €150,000”
co_benefits: Potential energy cost savings, improved local air quality, improved energy security/reliability, but not specified as objectives.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as procurement of consultancy/engineering services, but the dominant function is planning/technical guidance rather than a procurement requirement imposed on others.
